result.markdown:
# Reshaping data

We cover two reshaping operations — `pivot()` to convert long data to wide format, and `melt()` to go back the other way — since the shape of a DataFrame directly affects how easy it is to plot.

{{< video https://vimeo.com/1195996941/bb8790c15e?fl=ml&fe=ec >}}

```python
import pandas as pd

# ── The gapminder dataset ────────────────────────────────────

url       = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/gapminderDataFiveYear.csv"
gapminder = pd.read_csv(url)

gapminder.shape
gapminder.head()
gapminder.info()

gapminder["continent"].unique()
gapminder["country"].nunique()
gapminder["year"].unique()

gapminder.to_csv("data/gapminder.csv", index=False)
print("Saved to data/gapminder.csv")

# ── Wide vs long format ──────────────────────────────────────

# Select 4 countries and 4 years
countries = ["Norway", "Brazil", "Nigeria", "China"]
years     = [1952, 1972, 1992, 2007]

subset = gapminder[
    (gapminder["country"].isin(countries)) &
    (gapminder["year"].isin(years))
]

subset

# ── Converting long to wide with .pivot() ────────────────────

# pivot() reshapes long data into wide format
#   index   = column whose values become row labels
#   columns = column whose values become new column headers
#   values  = column whose values fill the cells
wide = subset.pivot(index="country", columns="year", values="lifeExp").reset_index()

wide

# ── Converting wide to long with .melt() ────────────────────

# melt() reshapes wide data into long format
#   id_vars    = columns to keep as-is
#   var_name   = name for the new column of old column headers
#   value_name = name for the new column of values
long = wide.melt(id_vars = "country", var_name   = "year", value_name = "lifeExp")

long
```

## Exercises

### Exercise 4.2.1

For each of the three small DataFrames below, decide whether it is in **wide** or **long** format, and explain your reasoning in one sentence.

```python
import pandas as pd

df_a = pd.DataFrame({
    "country": ["Norway", "Norway", "Sweden", "Sweden"],
    "year":    [2000, 2010, 2000, 2010],
    "gdp":     [36000, 60000, 34000, 52000]
})

df_b = pd.DataFrame({
    "country": ["Norway", "Sweden"],
    "gdp_2000": [36000, 34000],
    "gdp_2010": [60000, 52000]
})

df_c = pd.DataFrame({
    "student": ["Alice", "Alice", "Bob", "Bob"],
    "subject": ["Math", "English", "Math", "English"],
    "score":   [88, 76, 91, 82]
})
```

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

`df_a` is **long** — each row is a single country-year observation, and `year` appears as values within a column rather than as separate columns.

`df_b` is **wide** — each row is a single country, and each year has its own column (`gdp_2000`, `gdp_2010`). The same information as `df_a`, but reshaped.

`df_c` is **long** — each row is a single student-subject observation. Reshaping this to wide would produce one row per student, with `Math` and `English` as separate columns.

The general test: in long format, a variable like `year` or `subject` is stored as *values* inside a column. In wide format, those same values become the column *headers* themselves.

</details>

---

### Exercise 4.2.2

The `flights` dataset is a good example of data that is about as long as it can be — 144 rows, one per month per year, with a single `passengers` column.

```python
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights.head()
```

1. Use `.pivot()` to reshape `flights` to wide format, with `year` as the row index, `month` as the columns, and `passengers` as the values. Assign the result to `flights_wide`.
2. What is the shape of `flights_wide`? How does it compare to the shape of `flights`, and why?
3. The columns of `flights_wide` are not in calendar order (January to December). Use the following list to reorder them: `["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun", "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"]`

<details>
<summary>Hint</summary>

For step 3, you can select columns in a specific order by passing a list of column names inside square brackets, e.g. `flights_wide[my_ordered_list]`.

</details>

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

::: {#bc8436e3 .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")

# 1. Pivot to wide
flights_wide = flights.pivot(index="year", columns="month", values="passengers")
print(flights_wide.head())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
month  Apr  Aug  Dec  Feb  Jan  Jul  Jun  Mar  May  Nov  Oct  Sep
year                                                             
1949   129  148  118  118  112  148  135  132  121  104  119  136
1950   135  170  140  126  115  170  149  141  125  114  133  158
1951   163  199  166  150  145  199  178  178  172  146  162  184
1952   181  242  194  180  171  230  218  193  183  172  191  209
1953   235  272  201  196  196  264  243  236  229  180  211  237
```
:::
:::


::: {#cdce14ce .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 2. Shape comparison
print("Long shape:", flights.shape)
print("Wide shape:", flights_wide.shape)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Long shape: (144, 3)
Wide shape: (12, 12)
```
:::
:::


::: {#9f559b3c .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 3. Reorder columns chronologically
month_order = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
               "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"]
flights_wide = flights_wide[month_order]
print(flights_wide.head())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
month  Jan  Feb  Mar  Apr  May  Jun  Jul  Aug  Sep  Oct  Nov  Dec
year                                                             
1949   112  118  132  129  121  135  148  148  136  119  104  118
1950   115  126  141  135  125  149  170  170  158  133  114  140
1951   145  150  178  163  172  178  199  199  184  162  146  166
1952   171  180  193  181  183  218  230  242  209  191  172  194
1953   196  196  236  235  229  243  264  272  237  211  180  201
```
:::
:::


2. `flights` has 144 rows and 3 columns. `flights_wide` has 12 rows (one per year) and 12 columns (one per month) — the same 144 values, just rearranged into a grid instead of a long list. This is about as compact as this data can get: every month-year combination now has its own cell rather than its own row.

3. `.pivot()` sorts the new columns alphabetically by default, which is why the months came out in the wrong order — this is a common surprise the first time you pivot on a text column like month names.

</details>

---

### Exercise 4.2.3

Continue with `flights_wide` from the previous exercise, or recreate it if needed. This exercise reshapes it back to long format and checks that nothing was lost in the process.

```python
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights_wide = flights.pivot(index="year", columns="month", values="passengers")
```

1. `.melt()` expects the row index to be a regular column rather than the DataFrame's index. Use `.reset_index()` on `flights_wide` first, and inspect the result.
2. Use `.melt()` to reshape the result of step 1 back to long format, with `year` as `id_vars`, and set `var_name="month"` and `value_name="passengers"`. Assign the result to `flights_long`.
3. Compare `flights_long` to the original `flights` DataFrame: do they have the same number of rows? Sort both by `year` and `month` and compare the total passenger count in each — do they match?

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

::: {#469f9a6c .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights_wide = flights.pivot(index="year", columns="month", values="passengers")

# 1. Reset index
flights_wide_reset = flights_wide.reset_index()
print(flights_wide_reset.head())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
month  year  Apr  Aug  Dec  Feb  Jan  Jul  Jun  Mar  May  Nov  Oct  Sep
0      1949  129  148  118  118  112  148  135  132  121  104  119  136
1      1950  135  170  140  126  115  170  149  141  125  114  133  158
2      1951  163  199  166  150  145  199  178  178  172  146  162  184
3      1952  181  242  194  180  171  230  218  193  183  172  191  209
4      1953  235  272  201  196  196  264  243  236  229  180  211  237
```
:::
:::


::: {#94e014a6 .cell execution_count=5}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 2. Melt back to long
flights_long = flights_wide_reset.melt(
    id_vars="year", var_name="month", value_name="passengers"
)
print(flights_long.head())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
   year month  passengers
0  1949   Apr         129
1  1950   Apr         135
2  1951   Apr         163
3  1952   Apr         181
4  1953   Apr         235
```
:::
:::


::: {#635989d7 .cell execution_count=6}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 3. Compare row counts and totals
print("Original rows:", len(flights))
print("Reshaped rows:", len(flights_long))
print("Original total passengers:", flights["passengers"].sum())
print("Reshaped total passengers:", flights_long["passengers"].sum())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Original rows: 144
Reshaped rows: 144
Original total passengers: 40363
Reshaped total passengers: 40363
```
:::
:::


3. Both DataFrames have 144 rows and the same total passenger count, confirming that `.pivot()` followed by `.melt()` is a true round trip — no information was lost, only the shape changed. The row order may differ from the original since `.melt()` processes column by column, but the set of values is identical.

</details>

---

### Exercise 4.2.4

Load the full gapminder dataset and practice pivoting on a larger subset than shown in the video, using a different `values=` column each time.

```python
import pandas as pd

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")
print(gapminder.shape)
gapminder.head()
```

1. Filter `gapminder` to just the continent `"Europe"`. Pivot this subset to wide format with `country` as the index, `year` as the columns, and `lifeExp` as the values. How many rows and columns does the result have?
2. Repeat the same pivot, but this time use `gdpPercap` as the values instead of `lifeExp`. What changed between this result and the one in step 1, and what stayed the same?
3. Why can `index=`, `columns=`, and `values=` be treated as three independent choices rather than a fixed template? Give an example of a different valid combination of the three arguments using the gapminder columns.

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

::: {#77dcd117 .cell execution_count=7}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")

# 1. Pivot Europe on lifeExp
europe = gapminder[gapminder["continent"] == "Europe"]
europe_life_wide = europe.pivot(index="country", columns="year", values="lifeExp")
print(europe_life_wide.shape)
print(europe_life_wide.head())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
(30, 12)
year                     1952   1957   1962   1967   1972   1977   1982  \
country                                                                   
Albania                 55.23  59.28  64.82  66.22  67.69  68.93  70.42   
Austria                 66.80  67.48  69.54  70.14  70.63  72.17  73.18   
Belgium                 68.00  69.24  70.25  70.94  71.44  72.80  73.93   
Bosnia and Herzegovina  53.82  58.45  61.93  64.79  67.45  69.86  70.69   
Bulgaria                59.60  66.61  69.51  70.42  70.90  70.81  71.08   

year                     1987    1992    1997    2002    2007  
country                                                        
Albania                 72.00  71.581  72.950  75.651  76.423  
Austria                 74.94  76.040  77.510  78.980  79.829  
Belgium                 75.35  76.460  77.530  78.320  79.441  
Bosnia and Herzegovina  71.14  72.178  73.244  74.090  74.852  
Bulgaria                71.34  71.190  70.320  72.140  73.005  
```
:::
:::


::: {#151f8718 .cell execution_count=8}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 2. Pivot Europe on gdpPercap
europe_gdp_wide = europe.pivot(index="country", columns="year", values="gdpPercap")
print(europe_gdp_wide.head())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
year                           1952         1957          1962          1967  \
country                                                                        
Albania                 1601.056136  1942.284244   2312.888958   2760.196931   
Austria                 6137.076492  8842.598030  10750.721110  12834.602400   
Belgium                 8343.105127  9714.960623  10991.206760  13149.041190   
Bosnia and Herzegovina   973.533195  1353.989176   1709.683679   2172.352423   
Bulgaria                2444.286648  3008.670727   4254.337839   5577.002800   

year                            1972          1977          1982  \
country                                                            
Albania                  3313.422188   3533.003910   3630.880722   
Austria                 16661.625600  19749.422300  21597.083620   
Belgium                 16672.143560  19117.974480  20979.845890   
Bosnia and Herzegovina   2860.169750   3528.481305   4126.613157   
Bulgaria                 6597.494398   7612.240438   8224.191647   

year                            1987          1992          1997  \
country                                                            
Albania                  3738.932735   2497.437901   3193.054604   
Austria                 23687.826070  27042.018680  29095.920660   
Belgium                 22525.563080  25575.570690  27561.196630   
Bosnia and Herzegovina   4314.114757   2546.781445   4766.355904   
Bulgaria                 8239.854824   6302.623438   5970.388760   

year                            2002          2007  
country                                             
Albania                  4604.211737   5937.029526  
Austria                 32417.607690  36126.492700  
Belgium                 30485.883750  33692.605080  
Bosnia and Herzegovina   6018.975239   7446.298803  
Bulgaria                 7696.777725  10680.792820  
```
:::
:::


2. The row and column structure — which countries, which years — is identical in both results, since `index=` and `columns=` were unchanged. What changed is the values filling the grid: life expectancy figures in step 1, GDP per capita figures in step 2.

3. `index=`, `columns=`, and `values=` each independently answer a different question: what should identify the rows, what should identify the columns, and what number should fill each cell. Any column not used as `index=` or `columns=` can be used as `values=`, provided the `index`-`columns` combination uniquely identifies each cell. For example, `index="year"`, `columns="country"`, `values="pop"` would produce a table of population figures with years as rows and countries as columns instead.

</details>

---

### Exercise 4.2.5

When reshaping data, you sometimes want to keep more than one identifying column intact. This exercise practices that using `country` and `continent` together.

```python
import pandas as pd

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")

subset = gapminder[
    gapminder["country"].isin(["Norway", "Sweden", "Denmark", "Egypt", "Kenya"])
]
```

1. Pivot `subset` to wide format using **both** `["country", "continent"]` as the index, `year` as the columns, and `lifeExp` as the values. What does the resulting index look like?
2. Reset the index so `country` and `continent` become regular columns again. 
3. Melt the result of step 2 back to long format, using **both** `["country", "continent"]` as `id_vars`. Verify the result has one row per country-year combination, with `continent` correctly preserved for every row.

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

::: {#ae0b9f16 .cell execution_count=9}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")

subset = gapminder[
    gapminder["country"].isin(["Norway", "Sweden", "Denmark", "Egypt", "Kenya"])
]

# 1. Pivot with two identifier columns
wide = subset.pivot(index=["country", "continent"], columns="year", values="lifeExp")
print(wide.head())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
year                 1952    1957    1962    1967    1972    1977    1982  \
country continent                                                           
Denmark Europe     70.780  71.810  72.350  72.960  73.470  74.690  74.630   
Egypt   Africa     41.893  44.444  46.992  49.293  51.137  53.319  56.006   
Kenya   Africa     42.270  44.686  47.949  50.654  53.559  56.155  58.766   
Norway  Europe     72.670  73.440  73.470  74.080  74.340  75.370  75.970   
Sweden  Europe     71.860  72.490  73.370  74.160  74.720  75.440  76.420   

year                 1987    1992    1997    2002    2007  
country continent                                          
Denmark Europe     74.800  75.330  76.110  77.180  78.332  
Egypt   Africa     59.797  63.674  67.217  69.806  71.338  
Kenya   Africa     59.339  59.285  54.407  50.992  54.110  
Norway  Europe     75.890  77.320  78.320  79.050  80.196  
Sweden  Europe     77.190  78.160  79.390  80.040  80.884  
```
:::
:::


Passing a list to `index=` creates a two-level row index — each row is identified by the combination of `country` and `continent` together, rather than by `country` alone. This is useful because `continent` would otherwise be lost during the pivot, since it is neither the index, the columns, nor the values.

::: {#96e3e1bd .cell execution_count=10}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 2. Reset index
wide = wide.reset_index()
print(wide.head())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
year  country continent    1952    1957    1962    1967    1972    1977  \
0     Denmark    Europe  70.780  71.810  72.350  72.960  73.470  74.690   
1       Egypt    Africa  41.893  44.444  46.992  49.293  51.137  53.319   
2       Kenya    Africa  42.270  44.686  47.949  50.654  53.559  56.155   
3      Norway    Europe  72.670  73.440  73.470  74.080  74.340  75.370   
4      Sweden    Europe  71.860  72.490  73.370  74.160  74.720  75.440   

year    1982    1987    1992    1997    2002    2007  
0     74.630  74.800  75.330  76.110  77.180  78.332  
1     56.006  59.797  63.674  67.217  69.806  71.338  
2     58.766  59.339  59.285  54.407  50.992  54.110  
3     75.970  75.890  77.320  78.320  79.050  80.196  
4     76.420  77.190  78.160  79.390  80.040  80.884  
```
:::
:::


::: {#a7a55b9e .cell execution_count=11}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 3. Melt back with two id_vars
long = wide.melt(
    id_vars=["country", "continent"], var_name="year", value_name="lifeExp"
)
print(long.sort_values(["country", "year"]).head(10))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
    country continent  year  lifeExp
0   Denmark    Europe  1952    70.78
5   Denmark    Europe  1957    71.81
10  Denmark    Europe  1962    72.35
15  Denmark    Europe  1967    72.96
20  Denmark    Europe  1972    73.47
25  Denmark    Europe  1977    74.69
30  Denmark    Europe  1982    74.63
35  Denmark    Europe  1987    74.80
40  Denmark    Europe  1992    75.33
45  Denmark    Europe  1997    76.11
```
:::
:::


The melted result has one row per country-year combination, and every row correctly retains its `continent` value. Passing a list to `id_vars=` works the same way as passing a list to `index=` in `.pivot()` — both let you carry more than one identifying column through the reshape.

</details>
